text: This unit follows Daniel and his friends being carried off to the court of the king of Babylon and being trained as some of his attendants. Space frames the action and is central to the ideological struggle of the chapter. The conflict is over where Daniel and his friends would worship. They worshipped Jerusalem and its Temple. The king of Babylon's court could be seen as being in far-off Shinar, the location of the Tower of Babel in Genesis 11, the most distant point from Jerusalem in the Bible and the location of some of the most foreign and heathen societies known to ancient Israel. The issue of worship is centred around names which the king imposed on all of the young men at the palace, names of his various pagan idols. Daniel and his friends request only a test of their ability to abstain from the palace diet, requesting instead food and water which would not be an affront to their God. By accepting their requested food and abstaining from drinking from the king's wine cellar Daniel and his friends create for themselves a "personal space" between themselves and the king which preserves the exclusive worship of YHWH as God of Israel, even in the most foreign of places, and safeguards their identity as members of the covenant community of Jerusalem and its Temple being the centre of that community in the world of Daniel. Through understanding the structure of Daniel 1 and the role of space in that structure, ancient Israelites/Jews could understand the conflict of Daniel 1 and appreciate the solution worked out by Daniel and his friends, the solution which allowed them to live as exiles in the strange land of the Medes while remaining faithful to the God of Jerusalem and the Temple.
